volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Braggart Soldier (Section 3.1.695 - Section 3.1.807b)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Miles Gloriosus, or The Braggart Captain

sections.label: Section 3.1.695
sections.original: tum plicatricem clementer non potest quin munerem ;
sections.translation: besides , ’ tis impossible for me , in civility , not to fee the expiating woman ; for long has [*] the mattress-maker [*] been grumbling , because she has received nothing ; besides , the midwife found fault with me , that too little had been sent for her . What ! arn ’ t you going to send something to the nurse that brings up the young slaves [*] ? It ’ s a shame if nothing ’ s sent her ; with what a brow [*] she does look at me . These and many other expenses of the women like to these

sections.label: Section 3.1.700
sections.original: me uxore prohibent , mihi quae huius similes sermones serat .
sections.translation: frighten me from a wife , to be uttering speeches to me like to this .

sections.label: Section 3.1.701
sections.original: Di tibi propitii sunt , nam hercle si istam semel amiseris
sections.translation: In good sooth , the Gods are propitious to you ; for so soon as you lose this liberty , you will not easily reinstate yourself in the same condition .

sections.label: Section 3.1.703
sections.original: At illa laus est , magno in genere et in divitiis maxumis
sections.translation: You are a person who are able to counsel wisely both for another and for yourself . But ’ tis some merit for a man of noble family and of ample wealth to rear children — a memorial of his race and of himself .

sections.label: Section 3.1.705
sections.original: Quando habeo multos cognatos , quid opus est mihi liberis ?
sections.translation: Since I have many relations , what need have I of children ? Now I live well and happily , and as I like , and as contents my feelings . For I shall bequeath my property to my relations , and divide it among them . These , like children , pay attentions to me ; they come to see how I do , or what I want ; before it is daybreak they are with me ; they make inquiry how I have enjoyed my sleep in the night .

sections.label: Section 3.1.710
sections.original: eos pro liberis habebo , qui mihi mittunt munera .
sections.translation: Them will I have for children who are ever sending presents to me . Are they sacrificing — they give a greater part of it to me than to themselves ; they take me home with them to share the entrails [*] ; they invite me to their houses to breakfast and to dinner . He thinks himself most unfortunate , who has sent but very little to me . They vie with one another with their presents ; I say in a low voice to myself :

sections.label: Section 3.1.715
sections.original: bona mea inhiant , me certatim nutricant et munerant .
sections.translation: They are gaping after my property ; while , in their emulation , they are nourishing me and loading me with presents .

sections.label: Section 3.1.716
sections.original: Nimis bona ratione nimiumque ad te te et tuam vitam habes :
sections.translation: Upon right good grounds and right well do you fully understand yourself and your own interests , and if you are happy , sons twofold and threefold have you .

sections.label: Section 3.1.718
sections.original: Pol si habuissem , satis cepissem miseriarum e liberis :
sections.translation: Troth , if I had had them , enough anxiety should I have had from my children .

sections.label: Section 3.1.719_720
sections.original: continuo excruciarer animi : si ei forte fuisset febris ,
sections.translation: ⋯ I should have been everlastingly tormented in mind ; but if perchance one had had a fever , I think I should have died . Or if one , in liquor , had tumbled anywhere from his horse , I should have been afraid that he had broken his legs or neck on that occasion .

sections.label: Section 3.1.723
sections.original: Huic homini dignum est divitias esse et diu vitam dari ,
sections.translation: ’ Tis right that riches should come , and that long life should be granted to this man , who both husbands his property and yet enjoys himself and has kind wishes for his friends .

sections.label: Section 3.1.725
sections.original: O lepidum caput . ita me di deaeque ament , aequom fuit
sections.translation: O what a delightful person ! So may the Gods and Goddesses prosper me , ’ twere right the Deities should so ordain that all should not live after one rule as to the duration of life . Just as he who is a trusty market-officer [*] sets their prices on the wares ; as that which is good or valuable is sold according to its excellence , and that which is worthless , according to the faultiness of the commodity , deprives its owner of its price ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.730
sections.original: itidem divos dispertisse vitam humanam aequom fuit :
sections.translation: so were it right that the Gods should portion out the life of man , so as to give to him who is kindly disposed a long life , and speedily to deprive of existence those who are reprobate and wicked . If they had provided this , bad men would both have been fewer , and with less hardihood would they do their wicked deeds ; and then ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.735
sections.original: quí homines probi essent , esset is annona vilior .
sections.translation: those who were good men , of them there would have been a more plenteous harvest .

sections.label: Section 3.1.736
sections.original: Qui deorum consilia culpet , stultus inscitusque sit ,
sections.translation: He who would blame the ordinances of the Gods must be foolish and ignorant . ⋯ At present we must at once have an end of these matters ; for new I want to go to market , that , my guest , according to your own deserts and mine , I may entertain you hospitably at my house , heartily and with right hearty cheer .

sections.label: Section 3.1.740
sections.original: Nihil me paenitet iam quanto sumptui fuerim tibi ;
sections.translation: I am content with [*] the expense that I have been to you already . For no guest can be thus hospitably entertained by a friend , but that when he has been there three days running , he must now become a bore ; but when he is prolonging his stay for ten successive days , he is a nuisance to the household . Although the master willingly allows it , the servants grumble .

sections.label: Section 3.1.745
sections.original: Serviendae servituti ego servos instruxi mihi ,
sections.translation: I have trained up the servants that are in my service , my guest , not to rule over me , or for me to be obedient to them . If that is disagreable to them which is agreable to me , I steer my own course [*] ; that which they don ’ t like must still be done at their peril , and whether they like it or no . Now , as I intended , I shall go to market .

sections.label: Section 3.1.749b
sections.original: Si certumst tibi ,
sections.translation: If you are resolved ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.750
sections.original: commodulum obsona , ne magno sumptu : mihi quidvis sat est .
sections.translation: do cater somewhat within bounds , at no great expense ; anything is enough for me [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.1.751
sections.original: Quin tu istanc orationem hinc veterem atque antiquam amoves ?
sections.translation: Won ’ t you now [*] have done with that old-fashioned and antiquated talk ? Now surely , guest , you are using the cant of the vulgar [*] . For they are in the habit of saying , when they have taken their places , when dinner is put on table : What necessity was there for you to go to this great expense on out account ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.755
sections.original: insanivisti hercle , nám idem hoc hominibus sat erat decem .”
sections.translation: Surely you were mad , for this same dinner was enough for ten persons . What has been provided on their account they find fault with ; they eat it up , however .

sections.label: Section 3.1.757
sections.original: Fit pol íllud ad íllud exemplum . ut docte et perspecte sapit .
sections.translation: Troth , in that self-same fashion ’ tis generally done . How clever and shrewd is his discernment .

sections.label: Section 3.1.758
sections.original: Sed eidem homines numquam dicunt , quamquam adpositumst ampliter :
sections.translation: But these same persons never say , although such an abundance has been provided , Do order that to be taken off ; do take away this dish ; remove this gammon of bacon , I ’ ll have none of it ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.760
sections.original: aufer illam offam porcinam , probus hic conger frigidus ,
sections.translation: put aside that piece of pork ; this conger ’ s good [*] when cold ; remove it , take and put it aside . You hear none of them saying this in earnest , but they stretch themselves out , while with half their bodies [*] on the table , they are indulging their appetite .

sections.label: Section 3.1.763
sections.original: Bonus bene ut malos descripsit mores .
sections.translation: How cleverly the good soul has described their bad manners .

sections.label: Section 3.1.763b
sections.original: Haud centesimam
sections.translation: I have not said a hundredth part of what I could have enlarged upon had there been leisure for the matter .

sections.label: Section 3.1.765
sections.original: Igitur id quod agitur , † hic primum praeverti decet .
sections.translation: The business , then , that we are about — to that we ought first to turn our thoughts . Do you both , now , give me your attention . I have need , Periplecomenus , of your assistance ; for I have hit upon a pleasant trick , how this Captain with his long locks may be fleeced quite close [*] , and how we may effect a means for Philocomasium , and this her lover ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.770
sections.original: ut hic eam abducat habeatque .
sections.translation: that he may carry her off hence , and have her as his own .

sections.label: Section 3.1.770b
sections.original: Dari istanc rationem volo .
sections.translation: I wish this plan to be imparted to me .

sections.label: Section 3.1.771
sections.original: At ego mi anulum dari istunc tuom volo .
sections.translation: And I , wish that ring of yours to be imparted to me .

sections.label: Section 3.1.771b
sections.original: Quam ad rem usui est ?
sections.translation: For what purpose is it to be used ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.772
sections.original: Quando habebo , igitur rationem mearum fabricarum dabo .
sections.translation: When I have got it , I will impart the plan of my devices .

sections.label: Section 3.1.773
sections.original: Vtere , accipe .
sections.translation: Take and use it . ( Gives him the ring .)

sections.label: Section 3.1.773b
sections.original: Accipe a me rusum rationem doli ,
sections.translation: Take from me in return the plan of my contrivance that I have hit upon .

sections.label: Section 3.1.774b
sections.original: Perpurigatis damus tibi ambo operam auribus .
sections.translation: We are listening to you with most attentive ear .

sections.label: Section 3.1.775
sections.original: Erus meus ita magnus moechus mulierum est , ut neminem
sections.translation: My master is such a shocking rake among the women , that I think no one ever was his equal , nor ever will be .

sections.label: Section 3.1.776b
sections.original: Credo ego istuc idem .
sections.translation: I believe the same as well .

sections.label: Section 3.1.777
sections.original: Isque Alexandri praestare praedicat formae suam ,
sections.translation: He boasts , too , that his beauty exceeds that of Alexander [*] ; and , therefore , he says that all the women [*] in Ephesus of their own accord are courting him .

sections.label: Section 3.1.779
sections.original: Edepol qui te de isto multi cupiunt † non mentirier ,
sections.translation: Aye , faith , many there are who could wish [*] that you were now telling an untruth about him .

sections.label: Section 3.1.780
sections.original: sed ego ita esse ut dicis teneo pulchre . proin , Palaestrio ,
sections.translation: But I am convinced full well that it is as you say . For that reason , Palaestrio , do compress your words in as short a compass as ever you possibly can .

sections.label: Section 3.1.782
sections.original: Ecquam tu potes reperire forma lepida mulierem ,
sections.translation: Can you , then , find any woman of agreable person , whose mind and body are full of merriment and subtlety ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.784
sections.original: Ingenuamne an libertinam ?
sections.translation: Free by birth , or bondwoman made free ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.784b
sections.original: Aequi istuc facio , dum modo
sections.translation: I consider that a matter of indifference , so that

sections.label: Section 3.1.785
sections.original: eam des quae sit quaestuosa , quaé alat corpus corpore ,
sections.translation: you find one who is greedy for gain , who supports her body by her charms , who has , too , her senses all awake ; as for her heart , that cannot be so , as none of them have one .

sections.label: Section 3.1.787
sections.original: Lautam vis an quae nondum sit lauta ?
sections.translation: Do you want one that has [*] taken her degrees , or one as yet a novice in the art ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.787b
sections.original: Sic consucidam ,
sections.translation: One sober but plump [*] , a juicy bit ; as taking a one as ever you can find , and one very young .

sections.label: Section 3.1.789
sections.original: Habeo eccillam meam clientam , meretricem adulescentulam .
sections.translation: Why , I have one , a dependant of mine , a courtesan , a very young woman .

sections.label: Section 3.1.790
sections.original: sed quid ea usus est ?
sections.translation: But what is the occasion for her ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.790b
sections.original: Vt ad te eam iam deducas domum
sections.translation: For you to bring her home at once to your house as your wife , and , for that reason , to bring her there dressed out , so that she may wear her locks with her hair arranged , and fillets after the fashion of matrons [*] , and may pretend that she is your wife ; so you must instruct her .

sections.label: Section 3.1.793b
sections.original: Erro quam insistas viam .
sections.translation: I am at a loss what road you are taking .

sections.label: Section 3.1.794
sections.original: At scietis . séd ecquae ancillast illi ?
sections.translation: Well , you shall know . But what sort of a maid has she ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.794b
sections.original: Est prime cata .
sections.translation: She is a rare clever one .

sections.label: Section 3.1.795
sections.original: Ea quoque opus est . ita praecipito mulieri atque ancillulae ,
sections.translation: We have need of her as well ; so give your instructions to the damsel and her maid , to pretend that she is your wife and is doting upon this Captain ; and as though she had given this ring to her maid , then she to me , that I might deliver it to the Captain ; and I must be as though it were a go-between in this matter .

sections.label: Section 3.1.798b
sections.original: Audio ;
sections.translation: I hear you ; don ’ t stun my ears as if I were deaf .

sections.label: Section 3.1.800
sections.original: ei dabo , aps tua mi uxore dicam delatum et datum ,
sections.translation: I myself will go straightway to him ; I ’ ll say that it has been brought and delivered to me from your wife , in order that I might introduce her to him . He ’ ll be distractedly longing for her at home , a scoundrel that cares for nothing else whatever but intriguing .

sections.label: Section 3.1.803
sections.original: Non potuit reperire , si ipsi Soli quaerendas dares ,
sections.translation: If you had commissioned the Sun himself to search them out , he couldn ’ t have found , better than myself , two more cleverly suited for this business . Be of good courage about it .

sections.label: Section 3.1.805
sections.original: Ergo adcura , sed propere opus est . nunc tu ausculta , Pleusicles .
sections.translation: Take you every care then . There is need of despatch .

sections.label: Section 3.1.806
sections.original: Tibi sum oboediens .
sections.translation: I am all attention to you .

sections.label: Section 3.1.806b
sections.original: Hoc facito , miles domum ubi advenerit ,
sections.translation: Take care of this . When the Captain comes home , do you remember not to call Philocomasium by her name .

sections.label: Section 3.1.807b
sections.original: Quem nominem ?
sections.translation: What am I to call her ?
